title: Bentley Reveals Its First Electric Car Amid the Wreckage of the Luxury EV Market
gist: Bentley is launching Torcal, its first full EV (5m SUV, 300+ mile range, reveal 23 Sept 2026), into a collapsing luxury-EV market where Lamborghini, Ferrari, Mercedes, Audi and Porsche are all retreating; CEO Walliser is betting an evolutionary, non-flashy design and Asian demand can succeed where flashier rivals failed.

insights.claim: Lamborghini shelved its Lanzador electric GT in 2026 after concluding demand among its buyers is going almost to zero, signaling that even ultra-high-end EV appetite has evaporated.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A canary for the premium EV segment: brands with the strongest pricing power are pulling EV product, so watch for further luxury EV program cancellations across VW/Stellantis marques.
insights.quote: going almost to zero, if not to zero
insights.locator: The Graveyard Torcal Is Driving Into

insights.claim: Ferrari's first EV, the Luce, wiped billions off the company's market value within hours of its Rome reveal, and Ferrari has pushed its second electric model back to 2028.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Equity market punished Ferrari's EV pivot immediately; a template for how investors may re-rate luxury automakers that lean too hard into electrification.

insights.claim: Mercedes-Benz sold just 1,450 electric G Wagens in Europe through April 2025 versus 9,700 combustion versions, roughly a 1-to-7 rejection of the EV variant by the same buyers.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that faithfully electrifying an icon does not convert demand; discount conversion assumptions in luxury OEM EV volume models.

insights.claim: Audi discontinued the Q8 E-tron and closed its Brussels plant, citing a global decline in customer orders in the electric luxury class segment.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Plant closures indicate structural, not cyclical, retreat; monitor VW Group capex reallocation away from luxury EV lines.
insights.quote: global decline in customer orders in the electric luxury class segment

insights.claim: Porsche's 2025 group operating profit collapsed 93 percent to €413 million, dragged down by a €3.9 billion writedown tied to reversing its EV strategy.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Quantifies the balance-sheet cost of a luxury EV strategy reversal; a comp for sizing potential writedowns at other premium OEMs including stablemate Bentley.
insights.quote: group operating profit collapsing 93 percent to €413 million

insights.claim: Porsche Taycan depreciation is so severe that some dealers have reportedly refused to accept their own brand's performance EV as a trade-in.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Collapsing residual values are a leading indicator of demand destruction and hurt lease economics; a red flag for luxury EV total-cost-of-ownership.
insights.locator: The Taycan's depreciation is so eye-watering

insights.claim: Rolls-Royce Spectre sales are down 44 percent and the Mercedes EQS SUV is down by about the same, showing the demand collapse extends across the full ultra-luxury EV field.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Broad-based double-digit declines confirm the weakness is a segment problem, not a single failed model.
insights.locator: Rolls Royce Spectre sales are down by 44 percent

insights.claim: Bentley reported its seventh consecutive year of profitability in March with €216 million operating profit on €2.6 billion revenue and an 8.3 percent return on sales, but that profit fell 42 percent year on year.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Bentley is still profitable but sharply deteriorating; the EV launch lands as margins compress, raising execution risk on the self-funded transition.
insights.quote: €216 million in operating profit on €2.6 billion of revenue, an 8.3 percent return on sales
insights.locator: Will It Be Different for Bentley?

insights.claim: Bentley quietly cut 275 management and non-manufacturing roles to help fund converting its historic A1 building in Crewe into a battery-electric assembly line plus a new design center and paint shop.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: The EV transition is being self-funded through headcount cuts, signaling limited parent-group capital support and margin pressure ahead.

insights.claim: In 2024 Bentley pushed its target for going fully electric back five years from 2030 to 2035 under a renamed Beyond100 Plus strategy, will keep selling plug-in hybrid and combustion models alongside the Torcal, and does not expect a second all-electric model before 2030.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Bentley has already de-risked its EV bet with a decade-long single-model gap; models assuming rapid luxury EV mix shift should be discounted.
insights.locator: Bentley has also already hedged its own EV bet

insights.claim: Chinese demand is falling for Bentley, mirroring the broader Western luxury brand weakness in China.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: China softness is a key swing factor for luxury auto revenue; a recovery there is central to the Torcal bull case.
insights.locator: just as with other Western brands, Chinese demand is falling

insights.claim: Bentley's first-ever full EV, the Torcal, is a 5-meter-long SUV with a claimed range of more than 300 miles and a full reveal set for 23 September 2026.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: Sets the launch date and headline spec to benchmark against Bentayga sales and rival luxury EVs; the reveal is a catalyst to watch.
insights.locator: Bentley has a name for its first fully electric car

insights.claim: The Torcal replaces the front radiator vent with a solid wall of illuminated crystals inspired by the Continental T, a deliberately unsubtle statement that runs counter to the quiet-luxury trend.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A design gamble against prevailing quiet-luxury taste; reception will test whether bold EV styling still sells in the segment.
insights.quote: a solid wall of illuminated crystals
insights.locator: At the front, perhaps the most striking visual element

insights.claim: Bentley has chosen not to offer a separate passenger screen in the Torcal, breaking with other high-end manufacturers, and mixes physical buttons for important functions with OLED screens.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A retreat from screen-maximalism toward tactile controls signals a broader luxury-interior backlash against all-glass cockpits.
insights.locator: Once inside, thanks to the all-round power doors

insights.claim: Walliser argues early EVs had to look radically different because opinion-making tech seekers wanted to signal they were advanced, but buyers no longer want that, making Bentley's careful, evolutionary, authentic approach right for the current moment.
insights.speaker: Frank-Steffen Walliser
insights.so_what: A bet that the failed EVs failed on styling, not electrification; if correct, evolutionary EVs from heritage brands outperform futuristic ones.
insights.quote: Now people don't want that. They just want to have a car, and this is why our timing maybe now is right.
insights.locator: Technology seekers that consider themselves opinion makers

insights.claim: Walliser calls the Torcal the most considered car in Bentley's history and frames the EV as an evolution rather than a revolution for the brand.
insights.speaker: Frank-Steffen Walliser
insights.so_what: Management is explicitly lowering the risk profile of the launch; sets expectations for a conservative, brand-safe product rather than a moonshot.
insights.quote: It's an evolution of the brand, and I think it's the right thing for Bentley to do—not look for revolutions.
insights.locator: The Graveyard Torcal Is Driving Into

insights.claim: The author is skeptical of Walliser's thesis, noting Mercedes designers felt the same confidence when they faithfully reproduced the combustion G Wagen in electric form, which still failed to sell.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: An evolutionary, familiar design is not a proven fix, as the electric G Wagen shows; do not assume Bentley's approach escapes the segment's demand problem.
insights.locator: It's a nice conceit, but I'm sure the designers at Mercedes

insights.claim: Ferrari's Luce, despite a $586,600 sticker and Western backlash, reportedly sold out its entire 88-unit first-year China allocation almost instantly, suggesting Asian wealthy buyers are receiving luxury EVs far more warmly than the West.
insights.speaker: Jeremy White
insights.so_what: A bifurcation signal: luxury EV demand may be geographically concentrated in Asia, so China/Asia allocation is the key metric for the Torcal's success.
